meta.notes: Populated from WayFinder app data and verified travel references. Null fields to be enriched via Tavily web search.

language_and_money.english_proficiency: Low — limited outside tourist areas, upscale hotels, and Caribbean coast. Creole English spoken on the Caribbean coast.
language_and_money.cash_vs_card: Cash dominant. Cards accepted at upscale hotels and larger restaurants in Granada and León. ATMs available in cities but unreliable — carry sufficient cash especially outside Managua.
language_and_money.atm_availability: Available in Managua, Granada, and León. Scarce and unreliable in smaller towns and Caribbean coast. Withdraw cash before leaving major cities.
language_and_money.tipping_culture: Not mandatory but appreciated. 10-15% tip in restaurants is generous and welcomed.
language_and_money.tipping_etiquette: Round up taxi fares. Tip tour guides $5-10/day. Restaurant staff appreciate 10% — not always included in bill. Hotel housekeeping $1/day.

visa_requirements.us_citizen: No visa required for stays up to 90 days (CA-4 agreement)

entry_and_border.passport_validity_rule: Must be valid for at least 6 months beyond date of entry
entry_and_border.tourist_entry_fee: $10 USD tourism card fee on arrival
entry_and_border.customs_restrictions: Strict limits on firearms, narcotics, and fresh produce. Declaration required for cash over $10,000 USD.
entry_and_border.duty_free_allowances: 400 cigarettes or 50 cigars, 3 liters of alcohol, gifts up to $500 value

health.malaria_risk: Present in rural and Caribbean coast areas. Low risk in major cities and Pacific highlands.
health.malaria_prophylaxis_info: Recommended for travel to Caribbean coast and remote jungle areas. Atovaquone-proguanil or doxycycline. Not needed for Granada, León, and Pacific coast.
health.dengue_risk: Moderate to high — present nationwide, especially in urban and lowland areas during rainy season (May-November).
health.altitude_sickness_risk: Very low — highest point (Mogoton) is only 2,107m. Not a practical concern for tourists.
health.food_water_safety: Tap water not safe to drink. Use bottled or purified water everywhere. Street food generally safe at busy stalls. Avoid raw shellfish from unrefrigerated vendors.
health.air_quality_concerns: Managua has traffic-related air pollution. Volcanic activity near Masaya can produce sulfur dioxide — check conditions before visiting.
health.pharmacy_access: Pharmacies available in cities and larger towns. Basic medications widely available OTC. Limited in Caribbean coast and remote areas.
health.medical_care_quality: Limited — private clinics in Managua (Hospital Metropolitano, Hospital Bautista) offer adequate care. Public hospitals underfunded. Evacuate serious conditions to Costa Rica or US.

risk_indicators: Authoritarian government with history of arbitrary detention of foreigners
Limited rule of law and due process
Crime in Managua and Caribbean coast
safety.crime_risk: Moderate — Nicaragua is historically safer than Honduras and El Salvador for street crime, but political risks are significant. Petty theft in Managua and tourist areas. Violent crime on Caribbean coast.
safety.scam_risk: Moderate — taxi overcharging, fake guides, currency exchange fraud
safety.transport_safety: Intercity buses generally safe on main routes. Avoid night travel. Chicken buses (repurposed US school buses) are very cheap but crowded. Roads can be poor.
safety.solo_female_travel_notes: Exercise increased caution. Catcalling and harassment more common than in Costa Rica. Avoid walking alone at night. Travel with groups when possible in less-touristed areas.
safety.lgbtq_notes: Same-sex relationships legal but no recognition of same-sex partnerships. Social conservatism prevalent. Public displays of affection not recommended. No legal protections against discrimination.
safety.night_safety: Avoid walking alone at night anywhere in Managua. Granada and León tourist centers are relatively safe until 10pm. Caribbean coast requires extra caution at night.
natural_disaster_risks: Volcanic eruptions (Masaya, Concepción, San Cristóbal — all active)
Earthquakes (Managua sits on major fault lines)
safety.political_stability_notes: Nicaragua is under authoritarian rule (Ortega government). Arbitrary detention of foreigners has occurred. Political situation is unpredictable. Monitor US State Department advisories closely. Avoid any political discussions or activities.
common_scams: Taxi overcharging — negotiate fare before getting in, no meters
Fake tour guides at major attractions
Currency exchange fraud — use official exchange houses (casa de cambio)
Unsolicited 'help' at bus stations leading to inflated prices
safety.police_reliability: Variable. Police can be influenced by political considerations. Tourist police available in Granada and León. Exercise caution in interactions with authorities.

weather_and_seasonality.best_time_to_visit: November to April (dry season) — best weather across the Pacific coast, highlands, and colonial cities
climate_zones: Pacific lowlands — hot and dry, distinct wet and dry seasons
Caribbean coast — hot, humid, extremely wet year-round, hurricane risk
weather_and_seasonality.rainy_season: May to October (Pacific and highlands); year-round on Caribbean coast
weather_and_seasonality.hurricane_typhoon_season: June to November (Caribbean coast — direct hurricane risk)
weather_and_seasonality.surf_season: April to October (largest swells on Pacific coast)

major_airports: Augusto C. Sandino International Airport (MGA) — Managua (only international airport)
transport.public_transit_quality: Managua has a public bus system but is confusing and slow. No metro. Motorcycle taxis (mototaxis) are common and cheap in smaller cities.
transport.intercity_bus_quality: Extensive network of chicken buses (repurposed US school buses) — very cheap but slow and crowded. Express minibuses (microlines) faster and slightly more expensive. Good coverage of major destinations.
transport.road_conditions: Main highways (Pan-American) paved and decent. Many secondary roads unpaved and rough. Caribbean coast roads very poor — rivers and flooding common obstacles.
transport.taxi_safety_notes: No taxi meters — always negotiate fare before getting in. Agree on price in advance. Avoid unlicensed taxis at night. Mototaxis are common and cheap for short distances.
ferry_routes: Granada to Isla de Ometepe (across Lake Nicaragua, ~1 hr)
San Carlos to Río San Juan (boat)
Big Corn Island to Little Corn Island (panga boat, 30 min)
transport.border_crossing_notes: Main crossings: Peñas Blancas (Costa Rica — busy, expect waits), Las Manos (Honduras), El Guasaule (Honduras). CA-4 agreement means stamp is shared across Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, Nicaragua.

budget.value_for_money: Excellent — one of the cheapest countries in Central America. Very good value for money, especially for food, transport, and accommodation.

connectivity.mobile_data_quality: Decent 4G in Managua, Granada, and León. Poor to nonexistent in Caribbean coast, Isla de Ometepe, and remote areas.
connectivity.wifi_quality: Available in most hostels and tourist-facing restaurants. Speeds variable (2-20 Mbps). Unreliable in Caribbean coast and rural areas.
connectivity.power_reliability: Frequent power outages (apagones) throughout the country — more common in heat of the day and during rainy season. Bring power bank. Larger hotels have generators.

best_areas_to_stay: Granada old town — colonial architecture, central, most tourist infrastructure
León colonial center — university city, revolutionary art, close to volcanoes
San Juan del Sur — Pacific surf and beach town
Isla de Ometepe — unique twin-volcano island in Lake Nicaragua
Little Corn Island — Caribbean gem, no cars, laid-back
Matagalpa — coffee highlands base, cooler climate
accommodation.peak_booking_season: December to April (dry season); Semana Santa (Easter) is extremely busy
accommodation.accommodation_notes: Granada offers the best range of quality accommodation. Semana Santa is the peak of peak season — book months in advance. Caribbean islands fill up quickly; book ahead.

local_customs: Warm and hospitable people despite political difficulties
Greet with a single kiss on the right cheek between women and mixed company; handshake between men
Family is extremely important — Sunday family gatherings are sacred
Strong revolutionary history — murals, sandinist symbolism common in León
Avoid political discussions — can be sensitive and even dangerous
culture.dress_expectations: Casual and relaxed. Cover up when entering churches. Modest dress appreciated in smaller towns and rural areas. Beachwear only at the beach.
culture.religious_sensitivity: Predominantly Catholic with strong Protestant communities, especially on Caribbean coast. Semana Santa is the most important week of the year.
social_norms: Punctuality is relaxed — being late is normal in social settings
Nicaraguans are proud of their country despite hardships — be respectful
Avoid any political commentary — neutrality is essential for tourist safety
Bargaining expected at markets but not in shops
culture.greeting_style: Warm — cheek kiss in social settings, handshake in business. 'Buenos días/tardes/noches' is standard.
culture.public_behavior_notes: Generally relaxed about PDA in tourist areas. Avoid any political symbols, flags, or activism — can attract unwanted attention from authorities.
culture.photography_etiquette: Ask permission before photographing people. Avoid photographing government buildings, police, or military — can result in detention. Some churches restrict interior photography.
important_holidays: Semana Santa (Easter week) — biggest holiday, beaches packed
Revolution Day (July 19) — Sandinista celebration, government-organized events
Purísima (December 7-8) — immaculate conception celebration, loud fireworks, street celebrations
legal_restrictions_tourists_often_miss: Photography of government buildings, police, and military personnel can result in detention
Any perceived political activism can lead to arbitrary detention
Drone permits required and heavily regulated
Collecting wildlife, plants, or shells prohibited

signature_dishes: Gallo pinto — rice and beans cooked together, the national breakfast staple
Vigorón — yuca topped with chicharrón and cabbage salad, served on a banana leaf
Nacatamal — large corn dough tamale stuffed with pork, rice, vegetables, wrapped in banana leaf
Indio Viejo — shredded beef or chicken stew with corn masa, tomatoes, and mint
Baho — slow-cooked beef with plantains and yuca, wrapped in banana leaves
Carne asada — grilled beef served with gallo pinto, plantains, and cheese
Sopa de res — hearty beef and vegetable soup
Quesillo — corn tortilla filled with soft white cheese, cream, and pickled onions (street food staple)
food.street_food_safety: Generally safe at busy, high-turnover stalls. Quesillo from street vendors is a classic and generally safe. Avoid raw seafood from unrefrigerated vendors. Stick to hot, freshly cooked items.
food.vegetarian_friendliness: Limited — meat is central to Nicaraguan cuisine. Gallo pinto, rice, beans, and plantains are vegetarian-friendly staples. Few dedicated vegetarian restaurants outside Granada and León.
food.vegan_friendliness: Challenging — dairy and meat are prevalent. Growing options in Granada and León for longer stays. Rural areas very difficult for vegans.
must_try_drinks: Flor de Caña rum — Nicaragua's world-famous aged rum, among the best in the Caribbean basin
Tona and Victoria beer — national beers, light and refreshing
Pinolillo — toasted corn and cacao drink, the national beverage
Chicha bruja — fermented corn drink (Caribbean coast)
Tiste — cacao and corn cold drink
Fresh tropical juices (maracuyá, tamarind, jocote, nancite)
regional_specialties: Caribbean coast: rice and beans in coconut milk, rondón (seafood/meat coconut stew), lobster
Highlands (Matagalpa/Jinotega): coffee (some of the best in Central America), dairy products
Masaya: traditional crafts market with local foods, rosquillas
food.drinking_water_notes: Never drink tap water anywhere in Nicaragua. Always use bottled or purified water. Ice at restaurants is usually from purified water in tourist areas — ask if unsure.
food.alcohol_rules: Legal drinking age 18. No strict public drinking laws but excessive public drunkenness frowned upon. Alcohol widely available.

top_national_parks: Masaya Volcano National Park — most accessible active volcano in the world, lava lake visible at night
Mombacho Volcano Natural Reserve — cloud forest, excellent birdwatching, overlooking Granada
Bosawás Biosphere Reserve — largest rainforest north of the Amazon in Central America
Cerro Negro Volcano — youngest and most active volcano, famous for volcano boarding
Miraflor Nature Reserve — cloud forest, bird-rich highlands, community tourism
Cayos Miskitos Marine Reserve — Caribbean coral reef system

top_day_hikes.name: Masaya Volcano — Lava Lake Viewpoint
top_day_hikes.description: Drive or walk to the Santiago crater rim to look directly into one of the world's few persistent lava lakes. Active, steaming, and viscerally impressive. Night visits when lava glows are spectacular.
top_day_hikes.notes: Sulfur dioxide fumes can be strong — bring a wet cloth. 30 minutes from Granada. Nighttime tours highly recommended. Gas mask available at entrance.

top_day_hikes.name: Cerro Negro Volcano Boarding
top_day_hikes.description: Hike up the youngest and most active volcano in Central America (728m) then board down the black volcanic ash slope at high speed. Iconic León activity. Stark, black moonscape landscape.
top_day_hikes.notes: Board and protective gear provided by tour operators in León (~$30). Hike up takes 45-60 min on loose ash. Descent takes 2-5 minutes depending on speed. Check volcanic activity before going.

top_day_hikes.name: Mombacho Volcano Cloud Forest
top_day_hikes.description: Hike or take a truck up to the cloud forest reserve atop Mombacho volcano with stunning views over Lake Nicaragua and Las Isletas. Excellent birdwatching — Mombacho salamander endemic here.
top_day_hikes.notes: Truck available to summit ($8). Multiple trail loops of varying difficulty. Zip-line available. Views often obscured by cloud — arrive early morning for clearest skies.

top_day_hikes.name: Concepción Volcano (Isla de Ometepe)
top_day_hikes.description: Challenging full-day ascent of the active Concepción volcano (1,610m), one of two volcanoes forming Ometepe Island in Lake Nicaragua. Dense tropical forest, volcanic rocky summit, spectacular lake views.
top_day_hikes.notes: Licensed guide mandatory. Very strenuous — 8-12 hours. Start at 5am. Bring substantial food and water. Not recommended for inexperienced hikers. Trail can be very muddy in rainy season.

top_day_hikes.name: Maderas Volcano (Isla de Ometepe)
top_day_hikes.description: Hike to the cloud forest-covered crater lake of the dormant Maderas volcano (1,394m). Howler monkeys, parrots, and if lucky, the rare white-faced monkey. Crater lake allows swimming.
top_day_hikes.notes: Guide recommended. Very muddy — waterproof boots essential. Start early. The crater lake reward is worth the effort. Rope descents in the final section.

top_day_hikes.name: Las Isletas Kayaking (Granada)
top_day_hikes.description: Kayak or take a boat through 365 small islands formed by a prehistoric Mombacho eruption in Lake Nicaragua. Howler monkeys, birds, and beautiful lake scenery.
top_day_hikes.notes: Tours depart from Granada waterfront. Kayak rentals available. Combine with sunset tour for best experience. Watch for wind on open lake sections.

multi_day_treks.name: Bosawás Biosphere Reserve Expedition
multi_day_treks.description: Remote multi-day expedition into Central America's largest rainforest reserve. Pristine wilderness, indigenous Mayangna and Miskito communities, extraordinary biodiversity. Requires significant logistical planning.
multi_day_treks.notes: Must arrange with specialized local tour operator in Jinotega. Carry all supplies. Serious logistics — not a casual adventure. One of the most remote experiences in Central America.

multi_day_treks.name: Río San Juan Jungle Route
multi_day_treks.description: Boat journey down the historic Río San Juan from Lake Nicaragua to the Caribbean coast. Spanish colonial fortress (El Castillo), wildlife-rich jungle, caimans, and river dolphins.
multi_day_treks.notes: Boat-based journey. El Castillo is the historic highlight. Limited accommodation along the route. Check safety situation in Río San Juan before traveling.

volcanoes: Masaya (635m) — most accessible active volcano in the world, persistent lava lake
Cerro Negro (728m) — youngest and most active, famous for volcano boarding
Concepción (1,610m) — active, Isla de Ometepe, challenging hike
Maderas (1,394m) — dormant, Isla de Ometepe, crater lake
Mombacho (1,344m) — dormant, cloud forest reserve, Granada views
San Cristóbal (1,745m) — highest and one of most active, near Chinandega
Telica (1,061m) — frequently active, near León, accessible hike
waterfalls: San Ramón Waterfall (Ometepe) — 35m waterfall on Maderas volcano slopes
La Maquina Waterfall (Jinotega) — highland waterfall in coffee country
El Salto Waterfall (near Matagalpa) — accessible highland waterfall
hot_springs: Aguas Termales (near Tipitapa) — natural hot springs outside Managua
Hot springs on Ometepe — simple natural pools near Concepción volcano base
lakes: Lake Nicaragua (Cocibolca) — largest lake in Central America, freshwater sharks historically, Isla de Ometepe
Lake Managua (Xolotlán) — second largest, heavily polluted, not for swimming
Laguna de Apoyo — pristine volcanic crater lake, excellent swimming and kayaking
Laguna de Tiscapa — small crater lake inside Managua, historic viewpoint
Laguna de Xiloá — volcanic crater lake near Managua, popular weekend swimming
rivers: Río San Juan — historic jungle river connecting Lake Nicaragua to Caribbean
Río Coco — longest river in Central America, Honduran border
Río Tipitapa — connects lakes Managua and Nicaragua
wildlife: Freshwater bull shark (Lake Nicaragua — rare now)
birdwatching_hotspots: Mombacho Volcano Reserve — cloud forest species, endemic salamander-related avifauna
Miraflor Nature Reserve — resplendent quetzal, cloud forest birds
Bosawás Biosphere Reserve — extraordinary diversity, harpy eagle
Río San Juan and Indio-Maíz Reserve — jaguar, great green macaw
Laguna de Apoyo — waterbirds, kingfishers, herons
stargazing_spots: Isla de Ometepe — limited light pollution, volcano silhouettes
Mombacho summit — above cloud layer on clear nights
scenic_drives: Granada to Masaya — colonial cities, Masaya crafts market, volcano
León to Chinandega — Pacific volcanic chain, dramatic skyline
Managua to Matagalpa — mountain highlands, coffee country
Rivas to San Juan del Sur — Pacific coast access road

surf_spots.name: Playa Maderas
surf_spots.description: Nicaragua's most consistent and best-quality surf beach. Powerful, punchy beach break with both lefts and rights across a stunning cove. Consistent swell from April-October. Still relatively uncrowded compared to Costa Rica.
surf_spots.notes: Short boat ride or hike from San Juan del Sur. Surf camp scene growing but still uncrowded. Best at mid tide. Rip currents present — be aware.

surf_spots.name: Popoyo
surf_spots.description: One of Central America's great, under-the-radar surf destinations. Consistent, powerful reef and beach breaks. Multiple peaks over a long stretch of coast. World-class on a big swell.
surf_spots.notes: Remote location keeps crowds low. Growing surf camp scene. Several breaks in close proximity — explore all of them. 4WD helpful.

surf_spots.name: Playa Gigante
surf_spots.description: Small fishing village with a great beach break and a friendly, no-frills surf scene. Good for intermediates looking for a quieter alternative to Maderas. Turtle nesting on the beach at night.
surf_spots.notes: Rustic accommodation available in the village. Very local feel. Combine with Popoyo for a surf trip on the south Pacific coast.

surf_spots.name: San Juan del Sur — Main Beach
surf_spots.description: Protected bay with smaller, gentler waves. Best for absolute beginners wanting lessons. Main surfing hub with good infrastructure — more boats to better breaks nearby.
surf_spots.notes: Boat taxis to Maderas, Marsella, and Yankee beaches are the best use of San Juan del Sur as a surf base. Main bay is fine for beginners only.

surf_spots.name: Playa Colorado (Rancho Santana area)
surf_spots.description: Remote, powerful beach break on a beautiful stretch of coast within the Rancho Santana private community. Excellent, uncrowded lefts on good swells. One of Nicaragua's better-quality waves.
surf_spots.notes: Located within a private development — day access sometimes restricted. Some surf camps have access. Long left walls when conditions line up.

wildlife_zones.name: Indio-Maíz Biological Reserve
wildlife_zones.notes: One of the most pristine and biodiverse rainforests in Central America. Very remote — accessible via San Juan del Norte or Río San Juan boat. Requires permit and guide.

wildlife_zones.name: Laguna de Apoyo
key_species: Apoyo crater lake endemic fish (Amphilophus zaliosus)
wildlife_zones.notes: Pristine volcanic crater lake 30 min from Granada. Swimming, kayaking, snorkeling for endemic fish. Nature reserve status. Several lodges on the rim and lakeside.

wildlife_zones.name: Isla de Ometepe
wildlife_zones.notes: Biosphere reserve status. Two volcanoes connected by a land bridge. Excellent hiking, petroglyphs, and rural culture. Only a few thousand people live on the island.

wildlife_zones.name: La Flor Wildlife Refuge
wildlife_zones.notes: Major olive ridley sea turtle nesting site. Spectacular mass arribada events August-October. Night tours with rangers. Very near San Juan del Sur.

sports_and_activities.name: Surfing
sports_and_activities.notes: Nicaragua is one of Central America's best-value surf destinations. Consistent Pacific swells April-October. Boards $10-20/day. Lessons $30-50. Far less crowded than Costa Rica.

sports_and_activities.name: Volcano Boarding
sports_and_activities.notes: Unique activity invented in Nicaragua — board down active volcano ash slope at 80+ km/h. ~$30-40 including gear. Must-do León activity. All tour operators in León offer it.

sports_and_activities.name: Volcano Hiking
sports_and_activities.notes: Nicaragua has more accessible active volcanoes per capita than almost any country. Masaya lava lake is extraordinary. Telica night hike with lava views is a León classic.

sports_and_activities.name: Freshwater Kayaking and Lake Swimming
sports_and_activities.notes: Laguna de Apoyo is among the most beautiful swimming and kayaking spots in Central America. Crystal clear, warm, and pristine. Kayak rentals $5-10/hr.

sports_and_activities.name: Colonial City Walking Tours
sports_and_activities.notes: Granada and León are two of the best-preserved colonial cities in Central America. Walking tours of churches, murals, and markets are outstanding. León has the best revolutionary mural art.

sports_and_activities.name: Sea Turtle Watching
sports_and_activities.notes: Olive ridley mass nesting (arribada) events are spectacular. Night tours with rangers. La Flor is the most accessible from San Juan del Sur.

sports_and_activities.name: Rum Tasting
sports_and_activities.notes: Flor de Caña is world-renowned aged rum. Distillery tours available from $20. One of the best rum distillery experiences in Latin America. Near Chinandega.

sample_itineraries.one_week: Granada (2 days) → Masaya volcano (1 day) → León (2 days) → Cerro Negro volcano boarding (day trip) → San Juan del Sur/Maderas surf (2 days)
sample_itineraries.two_week: Managua (1 day) → Granada (2 days) → Laguna de Apoyo (1 day) → Masaya (1 day) → León (2 days) → Isla de Ometepe (3 days) → San Juan del Sur (2 days) → Corn Islands (2 days)
sample_itineraries.classic_route: Granada → Masaya → León → Cerro Negro → San Juan del Sur → Ometepe
sample_itineraries.offbeat_route: Managua → Matagalpa coffee highlands → Miraflor cloud forest → Estelí → Corn Islands Caribbean

practical_info.arrival_tips: Managua airport (MGA) is 12km from city center. Use official taxis from the airport ($15-25 to center). Most travelers pass through quickly en route to Granada, León, or the coast. Granada is 1 hour from the airport and much more pleasant to base from.
practical_info.airport_scam_notes: Only use pre-paid official taxis from inside the terminal. Unofficial drivers will approach you — ignore them. Agree on price before getting in any taxi.
practical_info.local_sim_where_to_buy: Claro SIMs available at the airport and city shops. Bring passport. $5-10 for a data plan. Coverage good in major cities, poor elsewhere.
practical_info.money_exchange_notes: USD widely accepted in tourist areas. Change given in córdobas. Use official exchange houses (casas de cambio) or bank ATMs. Street money changers offer slightly better rates but carry scam risk.

laws.drug_laws_strictness: Very strict — possession of any amount can result in arrest and imprisonment. Do not use or carry drugs of any kind.
laws.alcohol_laws: Legal drinking age 18. No public drinking restrictions in practice. Alcohol widely available.
laws.drone_rules: Heavily regulated — permits required from INAC (Instituto Nicaragüense de Aeronáutica Civil). Prohibited over populated areas, government buildings, military installations. Avoid flying drones near anything government-related.
laws.wild_camping_rules: Generally permitted in rural areas with landowner permission. Not permitted in protected reserves without authorization.
laws.protected_area_rules: Reserve entry fees vary. Guides required for some areas (Concepción volcano, Bosawás). No collecting plants, wildlife, or natural objects.
photography_restricted_sites: Military installations — can result in detention
laws.lgbtq_legal_status: Same-sex relationships legal but no partnership recognition. No anti-discrimination protections. Social conservatism prevalent. Exercise discretion.
laws.marijuana_legality: Illegal — possession can result in significant prison time. Zero tolerance in practice.
laws.prescription_medication_rules: Bring prescriptions in original packaging with doctor's letter. Some medications difficult to find locally — bring sufficient supply.

family_travel.car_seat_rules: Legally required but rarely enforced. Bring your own.
family_activities: Masaya Volcano National Park — accessible lava lake viewing
Laguna de Apoyo — safe swimming in crystal clear crater lake
Las Isletas boat tour (Granada) — howler monkeys and lake scenery
Isla de Ometepe — rural island life, monkey spotting
Corn Islands — safe Caribbean beach environment for families

accessibility.wheelchair_accessibility: Very limited — infrastructure is poor throughout the country. Cobblestone colonial streets, unpaved roads, and limited ramps make wheelchair travel extremely difficult.
accessibility.accessible_transport: No wheelchair-accessible public buses. Private vehicle rental with driver is the only practical option.
accessible_attractions: Masaya Volcano (partial — paved roads to crater rim)

outdoor_gear_shops: Limited options — basic gear available in Managua shopping malls
Surf shops in San Juan del Sur for board rentals and basics
Volcano boarding gear provided by León tour operators
